item.title: SCP-2351 Note
item.content:encoded:
Any interest in adding a photo of the note? I don't know if it would fit your vision exactly, but I made a version of the note for what I pictured if you'd like to check it out.

https://www.deviantart.com/themorbidmemories/art/SCP-2351-3-Note-738030981

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I am a big fan of calling out the Foundation on not doing enough "good" from time to time(whether it is warranted or not). We all have our morality/headcanon for the Foundation, but depending on their resources, could they do more to help people? Maybe, maybe not. Anyways, here's wonderwall…

https://youtu.be/7zxNVklLJic
This pretty much sums up the appeal of the Short Works contest. A neat, short article. That's it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
dankaar is correct.

It's been a while since the contest. I might as well post an explanation of the SCP.

+ Explanation
- Explanation

This SCP was meant to be an exploration into what the Foundation regards to be a threat, and how they respond to such threats, as well as a broader inquiry as to what is really dangerous within the Foundation universe.

We have three geese, completely ordinary same for a non-lethal anomalous effect that involves people acting goofy around them and having a bit of fun while doing it. The anomalous effect is completely harmless and actually enjoyable to the people whom it influences. Moreover, people do not recognize it as an anomalous effect, which means it cannot breach the secrecy the Foundation seeks to maintain, as no one except those already aware of anomalies could recognize it as such. In short, even if SCP-2351 were left uncontained, they would constitute no danger whatsoever.

The Foundation's reaction to SCP-2351 was, quite naturally, to contain it. The first two geese were captured in New York City between July 3 and July 8. Now, this tells us the time between the first capture and the second was five days, which doesn't tell us how long the Foundation was trying to capture them, nor do we know how long it took them to find the third instance. We do know, however, that the process of capturing this SCP has a "detailed timeline." So apparently it took some effort. It also means that at one point MTF agents were running around New York City chasing geese which pose no threat at all to anyone. I think this makes for a hilarious mental image.

By contrast, let's look at the other things in the addendum. As many understood, the numbers signify annual death tolls. The geese, of course, have killed no one and never will. On the other hand, we have two other SCPs, 682 and 173. These two are iconic for how scary and lethal they are. Yet, in terms of sheer death toll, the numbers do not scale past the hundreds.

Compare this to the diseases. Vaccines exist for all four of these diseases, which means that these diseases are preventable. Yet, combined, they kill over a million people annually.

The point has been made that if the Foundation did not contain 682 and 173, they would kill far more. This is undeniably true, but the question I think is relevant is how many more? Imagine if the number next to SCP-682 were from a single containment breach. This would mean that if SCP-682 breached containment every single day and killed that many people, he still would take over three years to kill as many people as measles does every year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Little bit.

I think AWCY? is referring to those three specific Canadian Geese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article. Though, based on the planes that have gone down to bird strikes, several of which have been Canada geese I would dispute their 0 fatalities.

…am I being too anal there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AWCY is about ART not about things wut look on you and kill you.
And I like this one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The funny thing is, I didn't like the AWCY bit because I was too able to predict it, and felt like rolling my eyes. But it made sense; I understood the message being sent immediately (that may just be the circles I run in, since a lot of people in the comments didn't get it, and that's fair), and thought it was a fairly good one, albeit pretentiously and immaturely executed. This does fit AWCY, who seem equal parts derpy as much as they are non-derpy.

I upvoted anyway, because my minor annoyance wasn't really that bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Holy crap, thank you so much for pointing that out, Michael. I cannot believe that was up the entire time of the contest.

Also, I've updated the article title on the listing of SCPs. As I said, there is a reason why they were geese and not any other kind of bird. Hopefully this will clarify that.

With the contest having ended at 3 AM this morning, I just want to thank you all for making both my first contest entry and my first SCP such a positive experience. When I hit submit, I was more nervous than I have been in a long time. My hopes were basically that it would provoke a discussion, and wouldn't get downvoted to deletion. As it turns out, quite an interesting discussion has emerged out of this, and the article did better than I hoped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliant, particularly because I've been known to make animal sounds at animals when I see them, including geese. The symptoms and aftereffects are exactly as this article describes, so heartily upvoted.

However, the correct spelling of hepatitus is hepatitis. I'm not comfortable changing this myself because ACWY is not known for their spelling, however, if the error is deliberate, it does nothing to improve the article, so my recommendation to the author is - correct it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote, although I initially considered no-voting because:
I did not understand the note without explanation, and with explanation AWCY seems hypocritical since they have created dangerous artworks.
On the other hand, Foundation agents running after geese while honking is a good image, so I decided to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"So what AWCY? should do is protest the lack of funds for worldwide vaccines."

... They are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is… a bit weird, IMO.

Why on earth should the Foundation contain three Canadian geese that make you quack (or whatever geese do) like them? Why spend money, time, space, people for this? Containing anomalies is fine, but this…!

The article spends a lot of words describing the effects of the anomaly… = makes you quack and feel good about it. Okay. Yeah. When I fool around and laugh about it, it makes me feel good too. In general, laughter makes you feel good. It is scientific.

As for AWCY?….
1) How can you 'contain' a disease? By finding vaccine for it… Admittedly, the vaccine was not found by the Foundation, but they are contained, or would be if enough money was devoted to containment… So what AWCY? should do is protest the lack of funds for worldwide vaccines. The Foundation cannot do everything to solve the problems of the world.
2) How many deaths would there be per year from SCP-682 and -173 if the Foundation did not contain them?

So… yeah. Weird and silly. I like both, but this is not my kind of weird, and this is not my kind of silly. Sorry! Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AWCY skips should be artistic, and this is a very artistic object. I can imagine a modern performance artist wanting to do something like this. That alone was enough for me to upvote.

And I want to thank the people upthread for helping to understand the note. Now my vote is solidified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading this thread, it confirms one of the reasons I love AWCY?: the discussion thread their articles always have. In every AWCY? scp there are people saying how brilliant it is, people saying how stupid it is, and people saying how that's exactly the point; and that's the kind of discussion that I imagine would also occur in-universe, and the kind of reaction AWCY? wants.
So yeah, it's all pretty funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, you brought up very valid points. To be honest, I do actually like AWCY? for the reasons you cited. I see AWCY? as embodying of a lot of the traits about most post-modern art that I hate: they're self-congratulatory, pretentious, hipster trolls who are deliberately as obnoxious as possible. However, when they're good, they bring a perspective to the discussion that has merit, and cannot be dismissed no matter how much you hate the messenger.

Yes, much of it is trolling against the status quo, but I find that useful as the AWCY? articles I find to be the best (the Idea of a Shark, the emaciated statues that summon children dying of thirst, the "miror") point out how the status quo is fundamentally terrifying. The Foundation seeks to protect normalcy. AWCY? exists to demonstrate how normalcy might actually be scarier than the very SCPs the Foundation seeks to protect us from.

But you're right to say that there's more to anart than this, and you are right to say that there are higher targets than the one I chose to aim at. I was toying around with some other SCP ideas for my next project, but I think I'll put them on hold, because you and Djoric have piqued my interest as to where this all can be taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I won't speak for others, but the AWCY? association was just one part of my downvote. I strongly suspect that I'm not the only one, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much for the link.

If people are going to downvote this based on the fact that it's an AWCY? SCP, then they're welcome to do so. Personally, they're my favorite GOI, and I made this in part because I wanted to see more from them. But to each his own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The essay is A Study of Anomalous Art.

I believe he says it's hypocritical just based off of the fact that AWCY? has regularly created anomalous objects, items, and exhibitions with primary intent to kill for art's sake, or that they, given their skills in creating anomalous art could probably vaccinate the world themselves, assuming they put some work into it.

I usually find discussions of what is and isn't "in-character" for any GoI, including the Foundation itself somewhat exasperating. Not only does every writer have their own personal canon(s) and headcanon(s), but there will always be an outlier. In the Foundation there will always be a sadistic douche, and in AWCY? there will always be one hint of an actual artist, or a poignant piece. That said, I understand the reasoning behind grouping together a group (obvs). With this piece, I think it could stand as an okay, if simple skip that's been so far dragged down by its relation to AWCY?. As just an anomalous piece of performance art, it probably would fare better.

Personally, right now, I think okay and simple are fine enough, especially for a first-time skip and short-works entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to clarify I was not trying to be confrontational when I asked. I appreciate any comments I get, including the criticism.

If I may ask, what did you find "hypocritical" about the article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m confused. Is your objection that you found article ham-handed about delivering a point, or is it that you found there was no point?

My objection is that it's a ham-handed attempt to teach someone a lesson. My further commentary was more about whether that intent is shared by the author or not. That's a distinction that doesn't really change my reaction.

Whether the "point" is valid from your point of view or not, the larger issues stand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“It seems like every AWCY? object is some sort of ham-handed attempt to teach lessons.”

‘The story becomes "goofy hipsters do a thing for goofy reasons." No larger implications, no ring of truth about anything, no engaging experience for the reader, no insight into anything. Just some stuff that happens.’

I’m confused. Is your objection that you found article ham-handed about delivering a point, or is it that you found there was no point?

Djoric, I would love to read your essay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One more thing that bugs me about this: how did they get kill counts for 682 and 173? There's all kinds of implications from that, and I'm not sure I like any of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't view it as a problem if your interpretation was the sitewide view of AWCY?. Like, I would still downvote pieces that rely on them because I don't find that concept very engaging, but I wouldn't consider it to be deficient writing, per se.

What I find to be a problem is that a lot of writers intend for AWCY? to be competent individuals with artistic merit, but end up in the place you're describing anyway. This piece, to me, is an exercise in that. I suspect we're of a similar mind on that point, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally rather like that view of AWCY. I like that they're hamfisted hipster moralists, I like that they're talentless hacks, I like that they are violent and pointless and hypocritical and all of that.

This doesn't mean that I want to see more of their articles, though. I do want to see more actual art scips, taking different views, doing different things. I wrote an in-universe essay on the subject of anomalous art, trying to expand that particular horizon.

There's a dichotomy in my head: If it's good art, it's not AWCY - if it's AWCY, it will never be good art. And yeah, it gets very tiring seeing more and more AWCY articles that rate as "just passable" - they aren't good enough as art to not be AWCY, but they are not bad enough to be AWCY and meaningful, and the writing is competent enough that it won't get deleted. That's an unfortunate side effect, and if there is anything this community has taught me it is that unfortunate side effects are omnipresent. Fix one and others spring up.

But I just love AWCY as obnoxious little shits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with all of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The object itself is fairly trifling and uninteresting, but that's constructed that way in service of the narrative, so let's leave that aside. The main thrust of this article is yet another exercise in "AWCY? fucks with the Foundation, because the Foundation is bad enough at its job that anomalous hipsters know of its existence".

First, making this a product of AWCY? brings all of the pitfalls of that site trope with it. I see a lot of commenters saying "finally, an AWCY? item that doesn't kill people," but that was never the main problem with how that GoI was typically portrayed. The main issue to me has always been that AWCY? are artists in the absolute crudest sense of the word. Can you imagine having access to media that bend or break the rules of reality as we understand it? The kinds of artistic visions that were previously impossible, but can now be realized? What would someone with any sort of talent and drive do with that? In most of the literature on the wiki, the answer to that is "lol I troll u". It seems like every AWCY? object is some sort of ham-handed attempt to teach lessons. Art reduced in essence to "you're not the boss of me, maaaan". It's obnoxious, and I think it leads to an extremely myopic view of what art is and who an artist is. Whether or not the piece in question kills eighty people or not.

This article is a continuation of that problem. This is barely art. This is hypocritical moralizing more suited for a political party than what's supposed to be an art collective pushing the boundaries of reality itself in the service of their craft. The inevitable response to this "well they're supposed to be that way," which I've always suspected is more post facto rationalization of an article's weaknesses rather than intent, but then why would I read about that? The story becomes "goofy hipsters do a thing for goofy reasons." No larger implications, no ring of truth about anything, no engaging experience for the reader, no insight into anything. Just some stuff that happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler
– hide block

Given the comment about all of them having vaccines, I thought it was also, or mainly, a comment about the anti-vaccination movement. Given that the Foundation goes to such lengths to hide/discredit the anomalous, why not discrediting that movement also?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The note says "Three Canada Geese."

More specifically, the Canada geese now in containment. They likely won't ever kill anyone. The referenced skips can be contained slightly better so as not to kill anyone either, and don't kill many now.

Preventable diseases kill millions every year. The Foundation has a bottomless budget, but doesn't spend a cent on that sort of shit (unless the disease is really weird).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't get it without the explanation in comments, so I'm gonna say this doesn't work. Also, it kind of really needs a picture of geese. I'm sure finding a creative commons one would be easy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd say more like "here's how many people die each year from each cause. You're containing 682, which last year caused 129 deaths; 173, that killed 14; and these geese, that never hurt anyone. Meanwhile, you're not containing these disease. What the hell, heroes?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Novote for now while I chew on this a bit more. I personally can't stand AWCY? as a GoI and the connection to this scip doesn't help it, imo. Also, I would debate that 0 figure in the note, given the number of fatalities caused by Canada (Canadian?) geese interacting with airplanes/vehicles. Yukla 27 was one of the more notable ones. So…yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, you are cool. Finally.

I applaud. THIS is what more anartists need to model themselves around. It doesn't kill. It doesn't maim. It doesn't traumatize. It makes you want to act like an idiot for a short time, and it carries a valid message.

spoilers
things to think about

As far as I know, there are no immunizations for 682 and 173. Thanks for the clarification on the diseases, it really did cinch this up.

This is one of my faves from this contest. That's a short list. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting that it would be geese, considering there are so many ducks in the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my kind of article. Its quirky, weird and makes one seriously think about the Foundation through sheer and cold numbers (which is ironic in hindsight).

Loved it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • An out-of-nowhere effect that is quirky.
  • I believe I have seen an SCP-682 and SCP-173 reference in that note, which I find to be lovely.

Cons:

  • The note does feel tacked on just to enhance the article, rather than seamlessly flowing into the rest of the piece.

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm guessing that what AWCY? is trying to say is that

Spoiler
– hide block

the Foundation should try to disprove things that don't exist that are actually harming people, rather than hide harmless (edit: or well, comparatively less harmful) things that do exist?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fear it would be bad form to give any kind of detailed explanation given that this is a contest entry, especially one whose defining feature is limited word count. Besides, either an SCP can stand on its own or it can't. If it can't, so it goes.

I'll say this: those diseases have something in common with each other. They weren't randomly picked.

Spoiler
– hide block

They all have vaccines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand this.

That they can totally make merely amusing things what don't kill you?

That this is a piece of anart that may be intended to be nonsensical?

I get both. Hell, AWCY? is my favorite GoI. But my question still stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hint: The choice of diseases and the choice of bird were both deliberate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That this is a piece of anart that may be intended to be nonsensical?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That they can totally make merely amusing things what don't kill you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What in God's name is AWCY? trying to prove with this?
